title: 11/18/19 Google Earthing Roman Region 9 Circus Flaminius

transcript.text: Hi, Livin' on Bold Review Log, November 18th, 2019, Monday, 8.51 p.m.
So, I'm working on, um, um, the, uh, Google Earthing, the, uh, Region 9 of Rome right now.
And, um, uh, it's, uh, I'm having, yesterday I had to trace columns all day long.
I had to do all of the columns for the Circus of Nero, which has two rows around it.
And then all of the columns for this other big temple next to it.
So, hundreds and hundreds of them.
Um, I thought that I had traced everything in Rome, but apparently I didn't.
There's lots that I hadn't traced.
So, I finished that today.
Um, I had a few more to do and I got up.
I was working until, like, 10 o'clock this morning.
I got up at, or I was working at, like, 6 in the morning.
I went and got an energy drink and then, yeah, I just kept working until, like, 10 or something.
Then I got up at, like, 4 or something.
Pretty much slept all day.
And I've been here working.
Um, so, I did, I finished the columns.
And then I looked at the, um, Pantheon.
And, uh, looked at the floor.
And, uh, usually when I, uh, um, you know, look at big temples and stuff, I, if the floor has a design, I try to put a mosaic, a, uh, an image on the floor.
They look great, um, on ground level and from, uh, far away.
It makes it look really realistic.
Um, it's like adding film to a cartoon.
Um, and, uh, so, um, I, uh, um, I, I was looking at the Pantheon and the floor and, um, there was no design or anything on the floor.
So, I, I wanted to know what the Pantheon looked like.
Because I remember I'd seen in tours, or maybe even I walked in it.
I don't, I went to Rome.
I don't think I went in the Pantheon.
But I've seen that there's a distinct mosaic on the floor.
And I wanted to see it and try to get an image of it on the floor.
And so, I Googled it.
And sure enough, there was an image of the Pantheon's floor.
And this, in the right shape and everything.
So, I put it right on the Pantheon.
And, um, but without thinking, I just started tracing over it.
Um, and I can't remember if I was going to do that originally or not.
I think I was just going to put the image on.
That would have been a lot easier.
But I, I started tracing over it.
And, uh, and then, so now I'm kind of committed.
Yeah, I'm going to, I'm going to build polygon floor of the Pantheon.
Um, which I've, I've never done it like this before.
But I can't because Rome is like this.
But it just so happens that right where the Pantheon is, it happens to be really flat.
Uh, because that's where the Pantheon is.
And that's how Google Earth read it.
And, uh, which means that I can build three-dimensional stuff and it'll be flat on the top.
So, I can build all of the designs of the mosaic of the Pantheon in the polygon tool.
Um, and I can, but I can have it elevated.
Um, so it's actually good.
It'll make the Pantheon look better on my Google Earth.
Because the front of the Pantheon has, um, like 15 stairs or something.
And they're actually drawn.
The person who drew the map in 1750, uh, drew the stairs at a third of a meter.
Which is pretty much accurate.
Accurate size.
All the other stairs were drawn at, uh, two-thirds of a meter.
Double that.
So, he kind of doubled up.
And so, it's not completely accurate.
So, the Pantheon's the only building in the entire map where the stairs are accurate.
And, um, and I saw there were a lot of stairs.
So, the Pantheon is clearly elevated.
Um, just like all those other temples, if you Google Earth, I mean you Google Earth, if you Google, uh, Roman Temple, you're going to see a temple elevated on its base up about three meters high.
You know, uh, and then the stores go, uh, the, the stairs go up.
And on the side, it comes out.
It elevates on the side of the stairs.
And then the temple's built on top of that foundation that's like three meters tall.
And the Pantheon, I think, was like that too.
Maybe even higher.
Maybe I'll put it at, like, higher.
Four meters tall.
But the interesting thing is, if you look at the Pantheon now, because I looked at this tour of the Pantheon.
This guy has this, there's this, uh, YouTube channel called, um, Pro Walk Tours, uh, Rome Virtual Walk 4K Part 3, Trevi Fountain to the Pantheon.
Because he's, like, got the whole, the whole Rome tour going on.
I, I watched him.
I actually traced off of this guy, I think it's this guy, doing the, uh, Colosseum.
And I did my tour of the exact Colosseum.
I wanted to, uh, Google Earth it later when I'm done.
I didn't want to do it too early.
I'm keep looking at myself.
I want to look at the camera.
So, um, so.
If you look at this guy's video and you walk across the, I think it's called the Trevi Plaza, um, or whatever plaza that is.
And then you step up onto the Pantheon, the level where the Pantheon is.
All you do is step up on a step that's, like, a tiny little step.
It's just one small step.
And then you're in the level of the Pantheon.
The Pantheon is on the same level exactly as the rest of Rome.
It's all the streets and plazas of Rome.
Um, I don't know how they, oh, but I guess it doesn't flood because they have, you know, they have the river under control now.
But, but back in the day, that thing was way up on a, on a thing.
So that's just, it goes to show you that 2,000 years, um, what 2,000 years will do to a city.
It makes the ground level rise, rise, rise, rise.
Another really good example of it, probably the best example, um, or another good example in Rome is, um, is the, uh, the tomb of Augustus.
If you look at Augustus and you see it, um, like the foundation, the area that it's around, it's way lower than all the streets around it.
Um, cause they preserve that, but then the, the, the city just built up.
Also, if you look at Herculaum, Pompeii and Herculaum that got destroyed by the, uh, Mount Vesuvius in 71 BC.
Um, um, uh, Herculaum is a section of the city that got covered and then they found it.

And so now it's like a museum, outdoor museum, but if you look at it, the ground level of the whole thing on Google earth, like if put turn Google earth on 3d, the ground level, it is a good 10 meters below everything around it.
So, so yeah, that's, it's, uh, interesting.
So that's why the, the, the, the Pantheon is the same, same level, but just picture the Pantheon back in the day, like 2000 years ago when it was made being way, way higher.
And then the streets being way below.
So that's trippy.
So anyways, that's how I'm going to build it because I see all those stairs and, um, and because I'm already committed to building it.
I mean, if I had known, maybe I wouldn't have, because I don't have to elevate it.
If I'm going to build stairs, I can just build the stairs individually and have them all stat on the same height, but then it wouldn't be realistic.
And I wanted it to be, you know, realistic.
So that's it now.
And the colors will, will call out at you more in the Pantheon floor.
Plus I've never done anything like that, that intricate of a floor.
So might as well do it with the Pantheon because the Pantheon is the most interesting building.
Um, one of the most interesting buildings in the world, because it's, uh, arguably the most interesting.
Cause like when you, when you go into it, it's like a, it's, it's literally a time travel machine because it's the one building in Rome that is preserved perfectly.
I mean, you can see the, the Colosseum and that's amazing to look at because you can walk around in it.
You know, that's actually to me, probably the most interesting building in all of Rome, but it's not, it's not preserved.
You know, all the, all the paint fell off of it and it crumbled apart.
All the iron fell out of it.
And it's just a ruin of what it was, but the Pantheon is still 100% functional.
Just as good of shape as the day it was built.
I mean, and the, and the, and the floor, the mosaic floor, I'm pretty damn, I'm pretty sure the mosaic floor has never been replaced since it was built.
Oh, I should, I should, I actually should re, re, uh, research that.
I didn't research that before doing the video.
I know that the, um, I know that the, uh, the copper, um, roof was taken off of it, um, caught by, uh, Justinian to fund his wars.
And, um, they, and he also took off the copper doors.
So they replaced the copper doors and they replaced the roof.
I think they replaced the copper doors in the, in the Renaissance and like the 1700s, but, um, or the 1500s when the Pope did it.
But, um, but the, the marble floor, I think is original.
I'm going to have to, but if it is original, it's amazing because you've got an original, I mean, cause that's how Rome looked.
When you're walking in the Pantheon, that's literally how Rome looked.
The entire city looked like, imagine the entire city looking like that.
So it's a time machine.
Um, and, uh, it's, uh, it's, uh, it's, uh, it's, I think it's amazing that we have that to, to use.
So, um, that's what I'm going to do today.
I'm going to build the Colise, the, um, the Pantheon and finished building the Pantheon, the floor and everything.
And then I'm going to, um, um, uh, the next thing is to, uh, uh, uh, yeah, I don't know.
Oh yeah.
The Column of Trajan.
I'm going to figure out how to build that in a good way too.
Cause that's, that's another 2000 year old column that still stands that had stairs that went all the way to the top.
I would love to walk to the top of the Column of Trajan.
Cause I mean, there's 2000 year old stair step up at top of a column of engineering.
I mean, it's just, it's, uh, impressive.
So, um, and I think he, he, he destroyed a whole hill in order to make that column.
The hill was as high as the column is.
And so that's the other thing I'm going to build.
So yeah, I've got some interesting stuff to build today.
Oh, I've talked for over 10 minutes.
Um, oh, I have a new visitor to my channel.
Dale Spencer is commenting again.
Hi, Dale.
Hey, if you want to comment on every single one of my videos, like you were before, I'm not going to block you this time.
I'll just keep trying to convince you that my work is, uh, is, is the perfect work for any, uh, uh, person who considers themselves to be of the elite of society to do.
And when it comes out, everybody's going to be addicted to it.
And if you're not, oh, I pity the fools who go, why would you want to look at Rome on Google earth?
If the past already happened, why would you even want to be interested in it?
It's like, okay, well, there you go.
Maybe we could put you in the human exhibit over at the zoo or something like that.
Give you a couple of bananas.
All right.
So I'm going to, um, work tonight.
Maybe I can finish the Coliseum today.
I'm super excited to see what I can make the whole thing look like.
Cause this is a new level of detail.
